title: ASSESSING THE QUALITY OF DATA FOR ANALYSING THE SEX RATIO AT BIRTH IN VIET NAM
abstract: Abstract Despite widespread concern about high sex ratio at birth (SRB) in Viet Nam, little attention has been given to the quality of the data sources on which SRB estimates are based. This paper reviews the key data sources available for SRB analysis in Viet Nam. A standard assessment framework is developed and used to assess the quality of three national-scale datasets from a population change survey, the health information system and the vital registration system. A comparative triangulation analysis is conducted to determine the consistencies and differentials in SRB estimates at the national, regional and provincial levels. A map of SRB at the provincial level is developed to demonstrate geographical variations. The analyses show the differentials in SRB estimates across the three datasets, and identify six northern provinces centred in the Red River Delta region that appear to have consistently high SRBs. Keywords: sex ratio at birth (SRB)data qualityViet Nam Acknowledgements The authors would like to thank the General Statistics Office, the Ministry of Health and the Ministry of Justice of Viet Nam for access to relevant data sources used in this paper. The research was funded by the Australian Leadership Awards (ALA) Programme of the Australian Agency for International Development (AusAID). Data collection cost was partially covered by the project, ‘Evidence for Health Policy Development in Viet Nam’, which was funded by the Atlantic Philanthropies. The annual Population Change Surveys were partially financed by the United Nations Population Fund in Viet Nam. There was no influence from these donors on the data analysis and report writing, and the authors take full responsibility for the views expressed in this paper. Notes 1. The authors have conducted statistical simulation to calculate the effect of sample size on 95 per cent CI, and have observed that the 95 per cent CI of an SRB estimate of 105 would range from 101 to 109 for a birth cohort of 10,000 births. 2. CBR of 18.6 per 1000 multiplied by the total population of 82 million in 2005. 3. Equivalent to 20–40 USD.
authors_text: Pham, Bang Nguyen; Rao, Chalapati; Adair, Timothy; Hill, Peter S.; Hall, Wayne
